standards.name: ISO/IEC 27001:2022
standards.issuing_body: International Organization for Standardization (ISO) / International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)
standards.summary: The leading international standard for information security management systems (ISMS). It specifies requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an ISMS, and includes a set of 93 security controls organized into four themes (Organizational, People, Physical, Technological). Certification is awarded by accredited third-party certification bodies.
key_controls: Organizational controls: policies, roles, responsibilities, supplier security
People controls: screening, training, awareness, disciplinary processes
Physical controls: physical security perimeters, clear desk, equipment security
Technological controls: access control, encryption, malware protection, vulnerability management, logging, monitoring
Business continuity management
Incident management and response

standards.name: ISO/IEC 27701:2019
standards.issuing_body: International Organization for Standardization (ISO) / International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)
standards.summary: A privacy extension to ISO 27001/27002, establishing requirements for a Privacy Information Management System (PIMS). It specifies controls for both data controllers and data processors and is explicitly designed to support compliance with GDPR and other data protection regulations. Organizations holding ISO 27001 can extend it with ISO 27701 certification.
key_controls: Privacy governance, accountability, and roles (Data Protection Officer support)
Data minimization, purpose limitation, and consent management
Data subject rights enablement: access, correction, erasure, portability
Data retention and disposal policies
Privacy risk assessments (PIPIAs / DPIAs)
Cross-border data transfer management
Privacy by Design and by Default controls

standards.name: SOC 2 Type II
standards.issuing_body: American Institute of Certified Public Accountants (AICPA)
standards.summary: An auditing standard for service organizations that defines criteria for managing customer data based on five Trust Service Criteria (TSC): Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and Privacy. A Type II report covers a period of time (typically 6–12 months), demonstrating sustained control effectiveness — not just point-in-time design.
key_controls: CC1–CC9: Common Criteria (COSO framework — environment, risk assessment, communications, monitoring)
A1: Availability — uptime SLAs, redundancy, disaster recovery
PI1: Processing Integrity — complete, accurate, timely data processing
C1: Confidentiality — data classification, encryption, access controls
P1–P8: Privacy — AICPA privacy criteria aligning to GAPP (Generally Accepted Privacy Principles)

standards.name: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
standards.issuing_body: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), U.S. Department of Commerce
standards.summary: A voluntary, risk-based framework for managing cybersecurity risk, widely adopted by organizations of all sizes globally. Version 2.0 (2024) adds a new 'Govern' function to emphasize cybersecurity governance and strategy, and expands applicability beyond critical infrastructure to all sectors. The framework uses six core functions: Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover.
key_controls: GV — Govern: Cybersecurity risk governance, strategy, supply chain risk management
ID — Identify: Asset management, risk assessment, risk management strategy
PR — Protect: Access control, awareness training, data security, platform security
DE — Detect: Continuous monitoring, anomaly and event detection
RS — Respond: Incident response planning, communications, analysis, mitigation
RC — Recover: Recovery planning, improvements, communications

standards.name: NIST SP 800-53 Revision 5
standards.issuing_body: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), U.S. Department of Commerce
standards.summary: The most comprehensive catalog of security and privacy controls for federal information systems and organizations. Rev. 5 integrates privacy controls directly into the security control catalog and applies to all types of computing platforms. It is mandatory for U.S. federal agencies and provides the control baseline for FedRAMP (cloud) and FISMA compliance.
key_controls: 20 control families including: Access Control (AC), Audit and Accountability (AU), Configuration Management (CM)
Incident Response (IR), Maintenance (MA), Media Protection (MP), Personnel Security (PS)
Risk Assessment (RA), System and Communications Protection (SC), System and Information Integrity (SI)
Privacy controls: Authority and Purpose (AP), Data Quality and Integrity (DI), Individual Participation (IP)
Three impact baselines: Low, Moderate, and High — over 1,000 controls total

standards.name: ISO 9001:2015
standards.issuing_body: International Organization for Standardization (ISO)
standards.summary: The world's most widely adopted quality management standard, with over 1 million certified organizations globally. ISO 9001 defines requirements for a Quality Management System (QMS) based on principles including customer focus, leadership, process approach, continual improvement, evidence-based decision making, and relationship management. It is industry-agnostic and applicable to any organization.
key_controls: Context of the organization: understanding stakeholder needs and organizational scope
Leadership: management commitment, quality policy, organizational roles
Planning: risk and opportunity management, quality objectives
Support: resources, competence, documented information
Operation: process controls, design and development, supplier management
Performance evaluation: monitoring, measurement, internal audit, management review
Improvement: nonconformity management, corrective action, continual improvement

standards.name: ISO 31000:2018
standards.issuing_body: International Organization for Standardization (ISO)
standards.summary: An international standard providing principles, a framework, and a process for managing risk in any organization. ISO 31000 is not certifiable — it is a guidance document applicable to any decision or activity in any type of organization. It provides a structured approach to risk identification, analysis, evaluation, treatment, monitoring, and communication.
key_controls: Risk management principles: integration, structured, inclusive, dynamic, best available information
Framework: leadership commitment, integration, design, implementation, evaluation, improvement
Process: scope definition, risk assessment (identification, analysis, evaluation), risk treatment
Risk treatment options: avoid, modify (reduce), share (transfer), retain (accept)
Monitoring and review: continuous tracking of risk landscape changes
Communication and consultation: stakeholder engagement throughout the risk process

standards.name: CIS Controls Version 8
standards.issuing_body: Center for Internet Security (CIS)
standards.summary: A prioritized set of 18 cybersecurity best practices (Controls) with 153 Safeguards, developed by security experts to defend against the most prevalent cyber threats. The controls are grouped into three Implementation Groups (IG1, IG2, IG3) based on organizational resources and risk exposure — IG1 is essential cyber hygiene for all organizations.
key_controls: CIS 1–2: Inventory and control of enterprise assets and software
CIS 3–4: Data protection and secure configuration
CIS 5–6: Account management and access control management
CIS 7–9: Continuous vulnerability management, audit log management, email and web browser protections
CIS 10–13: Malware defenses, data recovery, network infrastructure, network monitoring and defense
CIS 14–18: Security awareness training, service provider management, application software security, incident response, penetration testing

standards.name: Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard v4.0.1
standards.issuing_body: PCI Security Standards Council (PCI SSC)
standards.summary: A mandatory security standard for all organizations that store, process, or transmit payment card data. PCI DSS v4.0 (effective March 2024) modernizes requirements with a stronger emphasis on authentication, continuous monitoring, and targeted risk analysis. Non-compliance risks contract termination by card brands (Visa, Mastercard, Amex) and significant financial penalties.
key_controls: Req 1–2: Install and maintain network security controls; apply secure configurations
Req 3–4: Protect stored account data; protect cardholder data with encryption in transit
Req 5–6: Protect all systems against malware; develop and maintain secure systems and software
Req 7–9: Restrict access by business need; identify users and authenticate access; restrict physical access
Req 10–11: Log and monitor access; test security systems and processes regularly
Req 12: Support information security with organizational policies and programs

standards.name: COBIT 2019
standards.issuing_body: ISACA
standards.summary: A comprehensive framework for IT governance and management of enterprise IT, published by ISACA. COBIT 2019 aligns IT strategy with business objectives, ensuring IT risks are managed effectively and resources are used responsibly. It organizes governance and management objectives into five domains and is widely used by internal audit and board-level IT governance programs.
key_controls: Evaluate, Direct and Monitor (EDM): governance framework, benefits delivery, risk optimization, resource optimization
Align, Plan and Organize (APO): strategy, architecture, innovation, portfolio, budget, HR, risk, security, compliance
Build, Acquire and Implement (BAI): program management, requirements, solutions identification, change management
Deliver, Service and Support (DSS): operations, asset management, configuration, problem management, continuity
Monitor, Evaluate and Assess (MEA): performance, internal controls, compliance with external requirements

standards.name: ISO 22301:2019
standards.issuing_body: International Organization for Standardization (ISO)
standards.summary: The international standard for Business Continuity Management Systems (BCMS). ISO 22301 specifies requirements for organizations to prepare for, respond to, and recover from disruptive incidents. Directly applicable to financial sector resilience regulations such as DORA and NIS2 in the EU, CPS 234 and SOCI in Australia, and MAS TRM in Singapore. Certification is available from accredited third-party bodies.
key_controls: Business impact analysis (BIA): identifying critical functions and acceptable downtime
Business continuity plans (BCP): response and recovery procedures for disruptive events
Recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO) for critical services
Crisis communication and stakeholder notification plans
Testing and exercising: tabletop exercises, functional tests, and full-interruption drills
Supply chain and third-party continuity requirements and contractual obligations

standards.name: ISO/IEC 27017:2015
standards.issuing_body: International Organization for Standardization (ISO) / International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)
standards.summary: A code of practice providing guidance on information security controls applicable to cloud computing services, supplementing ISO 27001 and ISO 27002. It addresses the distinct roles of cloud service providers (CSPs) and cloud service customers (CSCs), covering virtual machine hardening, tenant isolation, shared responsibility, and cloud-specific incident response. Applicable globally to cloud-hosted deployments under GDPR, NIS2, and MAS TRM.
key_controls: Shared responsibility model: defining and documenting CSP vs. CSC security obligations
Virtual machine hardening: secure baseline configurations and golden image management
Tenant isolation: logical and cryptographic separation of customer environments
Cloud service customer asset inventory, classification, and lifecycle management
Privileged access management for cloud administration consoles and APIs
Cloud-specific incident detection, response procedures, and customer notification

standards.name: ISO/IEC 27018:2019
standards.issuing_body: International Organization for Standardization (ISO) / International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)
standards.summary: A code of practice for protection of personally identifiable information (PII) in public cloud services, supplementing ISO 27001 and ISO 27002. Establishes controls for cloud service providers acting as PII processors, aligned with global data protection regulations including GDPR, UK GDPR, PIPL, and PDPA. Addresses consent, data subject rights, transparency, and cross-border transfers in the cloud context.
key_controls: Consent and purpose limitation: cloud processing of PII only for customer-specified purposes
Data subject rights: access, correction, deletion, and portability support for cloud-hosted PII
Transparency: mandatory disclosure of sub-processors and data processing locations
Data return and deletion: mechanisms for customers to retrieve or securely destroy their PII
International transfer controls: mechanisms for lawful cross-border PII flows from cloud services
PII breach notification: cloud provider obligations to inform customers of PII-affecting incidents

standards.name: NIST AI Risk Management Framework 1.0
standards.issuing_body: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), U.S. Department of Commerce
standards.summary: A voluntary framework for managing risks associated with the design, development, deployment, evaluation, and use of AI systems. The AI RMF 1.0 (2023) organizes AI risk management into four core functions: Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage. Referenced by U.S. Executive Order 14110 on AI safety, and increasingly used alongside the EU AI Act and ISO 42001 as a global benchmark for responsible AI.
key_controls: GOVERN: organizational policies, culture, roles, and accountability for AI risk management
MAP: context establishment, categorization of AI risks, and stakeholder impact identification
MEASURE: AI risk analysis, evaluation, monitoring, benchmarking, and incident tracking
MANAGE: risk response, recovery, communication, and continual improvement of AI systems
Trustworthy AI properties: valid/reliable, safe, secure/resilient, explainable, privacy-enhanced, fair
AI lifecycle integration: embedding risk management from design through decommissioning

standards.name: ISO/IEC 42001:2023
standards.issuing_body: International Organization for Standardization (ISO) / International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)
standards.summary: The world's first certifiable management system standard for Artificial Intelligence. ISO 42001 specifies requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an AI Management System (AIMS). It addresses responsible AI development, AI-specific risk management, transparency, and human oversight. Can serve as a primary conformance pathway for the EU AI Act for high-risk AI systems, and is referenced by Singapore's AI Verify framework.
key_controls: AI policy: organizational commitment to responsible AI development and deployment
AI risk assessment: identification, analysis, and evaluation of risks specific to AI systems
Impact assessments: evaluating AI system impacts on individuals, groups, and society
Data governance for AI: quality, provenance, bias assessment, and training data documentation
Human oversight: mechanisms for human review, intervention, and override of AI outputs
Transparency and explainability: documenting AI capabilities, limitations, and decision rationale
Responsible disclosure: communicating AI system characteristics to affected stakeholders

standards.name: SOC 1 Type II
standards.issuing_body: American Institute of Certified Public Accountants (AICPA)
standards.summary: An auditing standard covering internal controls over financial reporting (ICFR) at service organizations. A SOC 1 Type II report attests that a service provider's controls are suitably designed and operating effectively over a specified period (typically 6–12 months). Required or expected by organizations subject to SOX (Section 404), and relied upon by financial institutions, payroll processors, and any service provider whose controls affect clients' financial statements.
key_controls: Control environment: governance, code of conduct, and management accountability structures
Risk assessment: identifying risks to financial reporting accuracy, completeness, and timeliness
Control activities: authorization controls, segregation of duties, and IT general controls
Information and communication: financial data integrity, error detection, and correction mechanisms
Monitoring activities: management reviews, exception reporting, and independent audit functions
IT general controls: change management, logical access, computer operations, and data backup/recovery

standards.name: NIST SP 800-171 Revision 2
standards.issuing_body: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), U.S. Department of Commerce
standards.summary: A NIST guideline specifying 110 security requirements for protecting Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) in non-federal systems and organizations. Forms the technical foundation for the Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification (CMMC) program required for U.S. Department of Defense contractors. Organizations must self-attest or obtain third-party assessment (C3PAO) to demonstrate compliance for DoD contract awards.
key_controls: Access Control (AC): limiting CUI access to authorized users, processes, and devices
Audit and Accountability (AU): creating and protecting audit logs of CUI system activity
Configuration Management (CM): establishing and maintaining secure baseline configurations
Identification and Authentication (IA): identifying and authenticating users and devices
Incident Response (IR): establishing operational IR capabilities for CUI systems
Risk Assessment (RA): periodically assessing risk to CUI operations and systems
System and Communications Protection (SC): monitoring, controlling, and protecting organizational communications

standards.name: HITRUST CSF v11
standards.issuing_body: Health Information Trust Alliance (HITRUST)
standards.summary: A comprehensive, certifiable security framework designed for the healthcare industry, consolidating requirements from HIPAA, HITECH, NIST CSF, ISO 27001, PCI DSS, and other standards into a unified, risk-based control set. HITRUST r2 Validated Assessment is widely accepted as evidence of HIPAA compliance and security maturity in U.S. healthcare. Increasingly adopted internationally by health data processors in Australia, UK, and the EU.
key_controls: Information Protection Program: governance, risk management, and compliance oversight
Endpoint Protection: antimalware, mobile device management, and workstation controls
Access Control: identity management, privileged access, and multi-factor authentication for PHI
Audit Logging and Monitoring: comprehensive logging of PHI access and security events
Third Party Assurance: vendor risk management and business associate agreement compliance
Incident Management: detection, reporting, and response to breaches of protected health information
Scalable assessment types: e1 (Essential), i1 (Implemented), r2 (Risk-Based validated)

standards.name: ISO/IEC 20000-1:2018
standards.issuing_body: International Organization for Standardization (ISO) / International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)
standards.summary: The international standard for IT Service Management (ITSM), specifying requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving a Service Management System (SMS). Aligned with ITIL practices and directly applicable to financial sector IT service providers under DORA and NIS2, it demonstrates that an organization delivers and manages IT services in a controlled, measurable, and continuously improving manner.
key_controls: Service management system: planning, governance, resource management, and policy alignment
Service design and transition: change management, release management, and service acceptance testing
Service delivery: capacity planning, availability management, and service continuity planning
Incident and problem management: detection, classification, resolution, and root cause analysis
Service request management: fulfillment processes with defined SLAs and escalation paths
Supplier management: third-party contracts, performance monitoring, and sub-contractor controls

standards.name: ISA/IEC 62443
standards.issuing_body: International Society of Automation (ISA) / International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)
standards.summary: A series of standards for securing Industrial Automation and Control Systems (IACS) and Operational Technology (OT) environments across energy, water, manufacturing, and transportation sectors. ISA/IEC 62443 is referenced by NIS2 (EU), CIRCIA (US), BSI Kritisschutz (Germany), and critical infrastructure protection regulations worldwide. It addresses security for asset owners, system integrators, and product suppliers throughout the OT lifecycle.
key_controls: Security Level (SL) framework: SL 1 (basic) through SL 4 (state-actor resistant), matched to threat profiles
Zone and conduit model: logical segmentation of industrial networks into security zones with controlled conduits
Component security requirements (SL-C): hardware and software security ratings for IACS products
System integration security (SL-T): security requirements for integrating components into complete systems
Patch management for OT: vulnerability management adapted for industrial uptime and safety constraints
Supply chain security: security assurance requirements for IACS vendors and third-party components
